stories.title: Meridian Arranges $186M Financing for Asbury Park Condo Project
stories.excerpt: Meridian Capital Group arranged $186,000,000 in construction financing for Lido Oceanfront Residences, a new luxury condominium development located at 1201 Ocean Ave. N. in Asbury Park, NJ, on behalf of Inspired by Somerset Development. The loan was provided by Madison Realty Capital and was arranged by Meridian’s David Bollag and James Darling. Lido Oceanfront Residences will deliver 112 for-sale residences ranging from one to four bedrooms, along with ground-floor retail space and an amenity program that includes a swimming pool, wellness spa, fitness center, private landscaped park, and resident lounges. The construction loan will fund the vertical development of the project. “Ralph Zucker and the team at Inspired by Somerset are among the most visionary developers in the region, and it was a privilege to arrange the financing for a project of this caliber,” said Bollag. “Our team moved quickly and worked through every detail of a complex, ground-up construction request to structure and close the loan on the timeline the sponsor needed.” The post Meridian Arranges $186M Financing for Asbury Park Condo Project appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Meridian arranges $186M construction loan for 112-unit luxury condo at 1201 Ocean Ave., Asbury Park, NJ
Madison Realty Capital lender; ground-floor retail, resort-style amenities included in for-sale tower
Condo financing still active despite multifamily focus; signals capital availability for coastal trophy assets
stories.summary: Meridian Capital Group arranged $186 million in construction financing for Lido Oceanfront Residences, a 112-unit luxury condominium development in Asbury Park, NJ, with Madison Realty Capital as lender. The project includes ground-floor retail and extensive amenities. The transaction closed on an accelerated timeline and reflects continued institutional lender appetite for high-quality, oceanfront residential development.
stories.impact: While this is a for-sale condo deal outside the traditional rental multifamily space, it signals strong capital availability and risk appetite for trophy coastal residential assets. Indicates lenders remain confident in high-barrier-to-entry markets with demographic and lifestyle tailwinds—a signal that Asbury Park continues gentrification momentum and could drive rental demand nearby if condo absorption softens.

stories.title: Prologis Beats Expectations With Record Leasing and Data Center Growth
stories.excerpt: The world’s biggest warehouse landlord is still full steam ahead on data centers. “After several quarters of sustained demand, we believe the market is entering its next phase,” Prologis CEO Dan Letter said in a second-quarter earnings call Thursday afternoon. The real estate investment trust (REIT) beat expectations in the second quarter of 2026, with record leasing and year-to-date data center starts already surpassing its full-year guidance. “The same land, customer relationships and operating capabilities that have made us the leader in logistics are enabling our data center and energy businesses, creating two additional long-term growth opportunities for Prologis,” Letter said during the call. The quarterly results come just six days before Prologis has a big decision to make. It must either make a formal offer to buy Segro, Britain’s biggest warehouse REIT and its international rival, or abandon its unsolicited bid. Segro’s board firmly rejected Prologis’ $16.6 billion all-share acquisition proposal in June. The company’s executives declined to take questions on the offer, but Letter told attendees that they’d put forward a “very compelling proposal.” “It offers a meaningful premium to where the stock has traded, and values the business above its stated [net tangible assets],” Letter said. “Importantly, it gives Segro shareholders the opportunity to participate in the upside of a stronger combined company, and the value of the Prologis enterprise and everything it offers should not be overlooked.” The news that the company leadership could share, however, was highly positive. Prologis reported core funds from operations (FFO) of $1.56 billion, up from $1.4 billion a year earlier and $1.44 billion last quarter. Net income reached $1.06 billion for the second quarter, nearly doubling from the $570 million reported in the same period of 2025, and up significantly from $980.5 million last quarter. Total revenue also grew annually, from $2.18 billion to $2.43 billi
bullets: Prologis posts record leasing; YTD data center starts already exceed full-year guidance
CEO cites sustained demand entering next phase; data center and logistics synergies unlock growth
Prologis pursuing $16.6B acquisition of Segro; portfolio rebalance toward tech infrastructure underway
stories.summary: Prologis beat second-quarter 2026 expectations on record leasing activity and accelerating data center deployment. The REIT has already surpassed its full-year data center start guidance. CEO Dan Letter emphasized that logistics, land, and customer relationships are enabling adjacent growth in data centers and energy. The firm is also pursuing an unsolicited $16.6 billion all-share bid for Segro, the UK's largest warehouse REIT.
stories.impact: Prologis' aggressive data center buildout signals sustained hyperscaler and AI infrastructure demand pulling industrial zoning and land away from traditional logistics and, indirectly, from multifamily development sites in secondary markets. In supply-constrained metros (Houston, Austin, Denver, Phoenix), this competition for developable land and scarce industrial-zoned parcels raises acquisition costs and reduces density of mixed-use/residential options. Multifamily developers should monitor land price inflation in AI cluster markets.

stories.title: Live Shopping Marketplace Storefront Takes 5K SF at 555 West 25th Street
stories.excerpt: Storefront, a new e-commerce platform and live shopping marketplace for brands and independent sellers, has signed a 4,700-square-foot lease at Mactaggart Family & Partners’ office building at 555 West 25th Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The length of the lease is for two years and the asking rent was in the high $30s per square foot, according to Michael Segerman from Current Real Estate Advisors, who represented both the landlord and the tenant in the deal. Storefront will launch this fall. “They’re growing very, very fast, and they’re probably going to be expanding within the building at some point as well,” Segerman told CO. Representing both the landlord and tenant also provided his firm the opportunity for greater transparency and equality for everyone involved, Segerman said. “It creates a fair deal for both sides,” he said. “ There are no hidden facts, we just cut to the chase, and it makes it much easier to get deals done.” It’s unclear whether the deal represents a new location or a relocation for Storefront, but it will join another new tenant at the Chelsea building. Susan Rodriguez Architects, an architecture and design firm founded in 2017, recently extended its lease agreement at 555 West 25th Street. The firm will maintain its 4,151-square-foot presence in the building, Segerman said. Cushman & Wakefield brokered that deal on behalf of the tenant. The names of the brokers were not immediately available. C&W did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Storefront e-commerce platform leases 4,700 SF at 555 West 25th St., Manhattan; $30s/SF, 2-year term
Tenant expected to expand within building; rapid growth trajectory signals strong venture funding
Office conversion/repurposing of Chelsea space to tech retail; broader office-to-commercial shift
stories.summary: Storefront, a new e-commerce and live shopping marketplace, signed a 4,700-square-foot lease in Chelsea's 555 West 25th Street at asking rent in the high $30s per square foot for two years. The broker noted the tenant is growing fast and may expand. Storefront is launching in fall 2026.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. This reflects selective office-to-retail/mixed-use conversion in Manhattan's High Line corridor, where ground-floor activation attracts younger, well-capitalized tech tenants. No signal of residential demand compression; if anything, strong tech tenant appetite for Chelsea offices indicates white-collar job resilience and potential rental demand nearby—but this is retail leasing, not residential.

stories.title: AI Firm Isara Laboratories Inks 6K-SF Lease at 515 West 20th Street
stories.excerpt: Isara Laboratories, an artificial intelligence startup platform, has taken 6,009 square feet at the boutique office building at 515 West 20th Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The company, which builds software so AI agents can communicate and solve problems for the finance and biotech industries, signed the lease June 20, according to broker Current Real Estate Advisors. The landlord is Justin Amirian’s Eagle Point Properties, which bought the Chelsea property from A&R Kalimian Realty for $23.3 million in 2022, property records show. The seven-story mixed-use building along the High Line is famously home to members-only club Soho House. Michael Segerman from Current Real Estate Advisors represented both the tenant and the landlord in the deal. “There’s been an AI boom among office tenants,” Segerman told Commercial Observer. “They’re not necessarily signing very, very long-term leases, but they are gravitating toward this West Chelsea pocket. They want something that is downtown and hip, but not priced like the SoHo market.” Sergerman did not share the specific asking rent, but he said office rents at 515 West 20th Street are in the high $30s per square foot. The length of the lease is for two years. Isara had set up a temporary office shop at 43 West 29th Street, which is still listed on its website. The company is in the process of moving into its new office space. A spokesperson for Isara did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. The firm’s “home base” for its engineering team is in London, while its commercial and customer teams are in New York, and its engineering and research hub is in Romania. Built in 1935 and renovated in 1985, 515 West 20th Street is a nearly 84,000-square-foot office property. Other tenants in the building include the Fred Astaire Dance Studio, which signed a 3,258-square-foot office lease at the property on May 1, according to Current Real Estate Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commer
bullets: Isara Laboratories (AI startup) leases 6,009 SF at 515 West 20th St., Chelsea; joined by Soho House
Broker notes AI tenant boom gravitating toward West Chelsea over SoHo; seeking downtown-hip without SoHo pricing
Early-stage AI firms preferring shorter leases, flexible space; Chelsea emerging as secondary AI office hub
stories.summary: Isara Laboratories, an AI software platform for finance and biotech, leased 6,009 square feet at the 515 West 20th Street boutique office building in Chelsea on June 20, 2026. The building also houses members-only club Soho House. Broker commentary highlights an AI tenant boom gravitating toward the West Chelsea pocket for its downtown cachet at lower cost than SoHo.
stories.impact: Signals secondary spillover of tech/AI job concentration from SoHo and FiDi into Chelsea, sustaining office demand there. AI startups' preference for shorter leases and flexible space suggests capital-efficiency consciousness—not wage weakness, but potential constraints on long-term occupancy commitments. For multifamily: West Chelsea's appeal to younger, well-paid AI/tech workers underpins residential demand; developer opportunity in high-end rental product (studios, 1BR) targeting 25–35-year-old tech talent with venture backing.

stories.title: Transamerica Pyramid Center Scores Leasing Success Under New Ownership
stories.excerpt: YODA PLC announced the completion of more than 113,000 square feet of new leasing activity at Transamerica Pyramid Center. The seven transactions have been completed since the beginning of 2026, including five since YODA acquired the redeveloped property in March from a SHVO-led ownership group.“The leasing activity at Transamerica Pyramid Center reflects the enduring strength of this asset amid the ongoing flight-to-quality for premium-seeking tenants,” said Chris Roeder, vice chairman at JLL San Francisco. “We’re consistently hearing from AI and growth-oriented companies that speed-to-occupancy is a top priority.” Building on the recent leasing successes at Transamerica Pyramid Center, YODA will introduce a new collection of spec suites in the fourth quarter of 2026 along with premium tenant amenities at Two Transamerica. Designed to meet growing demand for high-quality, turnkey office space, the suites will provide tenants with immediate occupancy opportunities within a highly amenitized environment, according to JLL. The post Transamerica Pyramid Center Scores Leasing Success Under New Ownership appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: YODA PLC completes 113,000 SF of leasing at Transamerica Pyramid Center, San Francisco post-acquisition
Seven transactions since Jan. 2026; five closures post-YODA March 2026 acquisition from SHVO partnership
Flight-to-quality dynamic; AI/growth-firm tenants prioritize speed-to-occupancy; spec suite rollout Q4 2026
stories.summary: YODA PLC announced completion of over 113,000 square feet of new leasing at Transamerica Pyramid Center in San Francisco since the start of 2026, including five transactions after its March 2026 acquisition from a SHVO-led ownership group. JLL noted tenants—particularly AI and growth-oriented firms—prioritize speed-to-occupancy. YODA plans to introduce spec suites in Q4 2026 with premium amenities.
stories.impact: San Francisco office market recovery now underway with flight-to-quality favoring signature, amenity-rich buildings. The AI-driven speed-to-occupancy demand reflects strong hiring and relocation momentum in the Bay Area tech sector. This will sustain or strengthen rental demand in SF and Oakland multifamily, particularly for flex, furnished, and corporate housing near CBD nodes—a headwind for long-term lease capture but tailwind for short-term rental and corporate housing operators.

stories.title: Greystar Sells Virginia Apartment Tower for $216M
stories.excerpt: Greystar, perhaps the largest multifamily landlord in the U.S., has unloaded another property, this time in Northern Virginia. Investment firm GID put down $215.8 million to acquire the 423-unit luxury apartment tower in Arlington, Va., and changed its name to Windsor Courthouse. The deal for the 20-story development at 2055 15th Street North is equal to more than $510,000 per unit. The Business Journals reported the transaction, citing data from CoStar. Berkadia arranged the transaction on behalf of Greystar. Neither Boston-based GID nor Charleston, S.C.-based Greystar responded to requests for comment for this article. Located next to the Court House Metro station, Windsor Courthouse was completed in 2023, and it is the tallest residential building in Arlington’s Court House neighborhood. It features 18,500 square feet of ground-floor retail leased to tenants such as French café Maman, YogaSix and Playa Bowls. Japanese restaurant Saki is set to open in another spot at Windsor Courthouse soon. Berkadia’s Brian Crivella, Yalda Ghamarian, Bill Gribbin and Jack Canepa handled the deal for Greystar. The acquisition expands GID’s portfolio in Northern Virginia, managed by its Windsor Communities subsidiary, which runs Halstead Tower by Windsor in Alexandria, Io Piazza by Windsor in Arlington and the Ridgewood by Windsor in Fairfax. Greystar also maintains a presence in the submarket after completing a 231-unit high-rise across the street last year. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Greystar sells 423-unit Arlington, VA luxury tower to GID for $215.8M; $510K/unit ($216M reported)
Windsor Courthouse; completed 2023, next to Court House Metro; ground-floor retail (Maman, YogaSix, Playa Bowls)
Large operator portfolio trim signals value realization; trophy transit-oriented asset recycled to smaller specialist
stories.summary: Greystar, one of the U.S.' largest multifamily operators, sold the 423-unit luxury apartment tower at 2055 15th Street North in Arlington, VA to Boston-based investment firm GID for $215.8 million, equaling approximately $510,000 per unit. The 20-story building, renamed Windsor Courthouse, was completed in 2023 and is located next to the Court House Metro station, featuring ground-floor retail.
stories.impact: Greystar's selective portfolio reduction in Northern Virginia reflects confidence in asset valuation and capital return—a sign institutional buyers still value trophy, transit-rich assets. The $510K/unit basis suggests Arlington's Court House submarket (Metro proximity, mixed-use retail, high barriers to entry) commands premium pricing; this limits development feasibility for new competing supply and signals scarcity value. Strong signal of continued multifamily investor appetite in DC-Northern VA; no adverse demand signal, only capital recycling among mega-operators.

stories.title: KLNB Represents Chinese Restaurant Concept in National Harbor Lease
stories.excerpt: KLNB announced that restaurant brand Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao has opened its new location at 180 American Way in National Harbor. KLNB Principal Josh Weiner represented Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao in securing the lease, in collaboration with Jerry Johnson of Bennett Williams Retail, who served as co-broker and master broker for the tenant. Tony Summers represented the landlord, Peterson Companies. The Michelin-recommended restaurant signed the lease in 2024 for the 6,253-square-foot space. The restaurant serves Northern Chinese cuisine, including signature soup dumplings and features a full bar service. “We began by evaluating the market based on Nan Xiang’s specific expansion criteria and toured numerous opportunities throughout the region,” said KLNB’s Josh Weiner. “We’re excited to help bring Nan Xiang to National Harbor and introduce this acclaimed concept to even more customers throughout the Washington region.” The post KLNB Represents Chinese Restaurant Concept in National Harbor Lease appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao (Michelin-recommended) opens at 180 American Way, National Harbor; 6,253 SF
Restaurant lease signed 2024; full bar, Northern Chinese cuisine; KLNB brokered tenant representation
National Harbor continuing dining/entertainment activation; mixed-use retail fill-in despite broader retail headwinds
stories.summary: Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao, a Michelin-recommended Northern Chinese restaurant, opened at 180 American Way in National Harbor. The restaurant leased 6,253 square feet under a lease signed in 2024 and features soup dumplings, full bar service, and signature Northern Chinese cuisine. KLNB represented the tenant.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. National Harbor's continued dining/entertainment retail activation signals sustained foot-traffic demand and mixed-use vitality—a modest positive for nearby residential values if the submarket benefits from cumulative amenity enhancement. However, this is a single restaurant opening in an already-mature mixed-use node (not a signal of new residential-driving employment arrival). Monitor broader National Harbor/Oxon Hill multifamily demand trends independently.

stories.title: Southern California Retail Investment Jumps 62% in First Half of 2026
stories.excerpt: Even though less retail space is changing hands in Southern California, commercial real estate investment firms are still pouring a lot more into the asset class so far in 2026. Thanks to slow construction and improving sentiment, retail investment sales hit $3.52 billion during the first six months of the year in Los Angeles, Orange and Ventura counties and in California’s Inland Empire. That marks an almost 62 percent increase compared to this time last year, according to a new report from NAI Capital. That jump comes despite a 28 percent drop in the square footage traded over the first half of the year. About 8.35 million square feet in the aforementioned regions sold to new owners by the end of June, compared to 11.55 million square feet in the first half of 2025. NAI Capital reported deals have shifted to higher-value properties, and attributed the jump in investment to buyers and sellers being more aligned on pricing. Completed construction declined 45 percent annually to just 473,008 square feet, while the development pipeline also declined 10.2 percent to 1.5 million square feet. L.A. County led in volume with $1.7 billion in retail property sales, which is up 64.6 percent over last year, across nearly 4 million square feet, according to NAI Capital. Asking rents rose 1 percent to $2.98 per square foot triple net. Propelled by a 3.8 percent vacancy rate, Orange County’s retail investment sales hit $976 million. NAI Capital said demand remained particularly strong for smaller, premium retail properties and coastal assets. The Inland Empire also saw a relative investment spike with a 142 percent yearly increase, while its Riverside and San Bernardino counties recorded a 9.3 percent increase in leasing so far this year. For the region, though, leasing volume declined 14.6 percent compared to last year to 6.23 million square feet. But overall retail vacancy improved to 5.9 percent from 6.3 percent, and average asking rents increased 1.1 percent to $2.36 per squa
bullets: Southern California retail investment hits $3.52B in H1 2026; +62% YoY despite 28% decline in SF traded
Only 8.35M SF transacted vs. 11.55M SF in H1 2025; completed retail construction down 45% YoY to 473K SF
Deals shifted to high-value properties; buyer-seller pricing alignment improving; construction/pipeline declining
stories.summary: Southern California retail investment sales jumped 62 percent to $3.52 billion in the first half of 2026 (Los Angeles, Orange, Ventura counties, Inland Empire), despite a 28 percent decline in square footage traded (8.35M SF vs. 11.55M SF in H1 2025). Completed retail construction plummeted 45 percent to 473,008 square feet. The shift reflects stronger pricing alignment on higher-value properties and a tightening construction pipeline.
stories.impact: Retail construction collapse in SoCal signals both structural headwinds (e-commerce, format shift) and scarcity value emerging for existing quality assets. Low retail supply growth opens opportunity for mixed-use multifamily projects with ground-floor retail to capture underserved neighborhood retail demand. In infill/urban SoCal markets (Santa Monica, West LA, Long Beach), constrained retail development suggests ground-floor commercial rents may rise, making retail-focused mixed-use development more economically viable—but residential rents must offset retail carry. Monitor Santa Monica, Pasadena, Culver City retail asks as bellwethers.

stories.title: Bradford Allen Closes On 25K SF of Leases with Illinois Retina
stories.excerpt: Bradford Allen has executed two recent leases with Illinois Retina in the Chicago market. The medical services provider signed a 16,228-square-foot lease in Skokie, Illinois and an 8,728-square-foot lease in Munster, Indiana, 30 miles southeast of Chicago. Craig Nadborne, executive managing director, brokerage services at Bradford Allen, negotiated the leases on behalf of Illinois Retina. “For more than two decades, Illinois Retina has trusted us to help align its real estate with the needs of its physicians, staff and patients,” said Nadborne. “Relationships of that duration are built over time, and these latest transactions reflect the group’s growth and continued commitment to accessible, well-located care in the Chicago-area market.” The Skokie office is located at Concourse Office Plaza, 4709-4711 Golf Road. The long-term transaction represented a renewal and expansion. The property comprises two 12-story medical office buildings. Illinois Retina’s Munster location relocated from a smaller facility to 9200 Calumet Ave. as part of a long-term lease. The post Bradford Allen Closes On 25K SF of Leases with Illinois Retina appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Illinois Retina signs 16,228 SF renewal/expansion in Skokie, IL; 8,728 SF new lease in Munster, IN
Medical office tenant 20+ year relationship with broker; reflects healthcare provider growth in suburbs
Suburban medical office outperforming; Skokie and Munster locations tied to healthcare talent/patient density
stories.summary: Bradford Allen executed two leases with Illinois Retina in the Chicago area: a 16,228-square-foot renewal and expansion at Concourse Office Plaza in Skokie, Illinois, and an 8,728-square-foot relocation lease in Munster, Indiana (30 miles southeast of Chicago). The medical services provider has worked with Bradford Allen for over two decades, signaling growth and continued suburban market confidence.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. This reflects sustained healthcare services distribution and talent clustering in Chicago-area suburbs (Skokie, Munster). If healthcare employment and in-migration into these suburbs is accelerating, it signals potential residential demand upside—but no explicit job growth or population influx data here. Monitor Skokie and Lake County, IN (Munster) for broader suburban employment and wage trends; if healthcare spending/employment is growing, multifamily fundamentals may tighten.

stories.title: JV of Synergy, Axonic Acquires Downtown Boston Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Locally based Synergy and New York-based Axonic Capital have acquired 265 Franklin St., a 21-story Class A office tower in Downtown Boston. The acquisition marks the first joint venture between the two firms. The seller was Clarendon Properties and the acquisition price was $116 million, according to the Boston Business Journal. “265 Franklin St. is an exceptional asset located at one of the most important intersections in Downtown Boston,” said David Greaney, Founder and CEO of Synergy. “Its institutional-quality construction, flexible floor plates, extensive recent capital improvements, and premier location create a compelling opportunity to continue enhancing the tenant experience while benefiting from the ongoing recovery and evolution of Boston’s office market.” Situated on a full city block bounded by Franklin, High, Oliver, and Batterymarch streets, 265 Franklin Street encompasses approximately 358,406 rentable square feet. The property offers immediate access to South Station, major MBTA transit lines, Interstate 90 and Interstate 93, as well as numerous dining, retail, and hospitality amenities. The post JV of Synergy, Axonic Acquires Downtown Boston Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Synergy and Axonic Capital JV acquires 265 Franklin St., Boston office tower for $116M; first partnership
358,406 rentable SF, 21-story Class A; transit-proximate (South Station, MBTA I-90, I-93); recent capex spend
Flight-to-quality Boston office recovery signal; institutional quality, transit, recent upgrades attract new capital
stories.summary: Synergy (Boston-based) and Axonic Capital (New York-based) formed a joint venture to acquire 265 Franklin Street, a 21-story Class A office tower in Downtown Boston, for $116 million from Clarendon Properties. The building comprises 358,406 rentable square feet, with institutional-quality construction, flexible floor plates, and extensive recent capital improvements. It is positioned at a major Downtown intersection with direct access to South Station and transit.
stories.impact: Downtown Boston office market shows institutional investor confidence despite ongoing sector headwinds. Acquisition of Class A, transit-proximate, recently-upgraded office signals flight-to-quality and densification trends that favor mixed-use and transit-oriented residential development nearby. Multi-unit transit access (South Station, MBTA lines, highways) and restaurant/retail amenities underpin demand for downtown residential—especially for young professionals and transit-dependent renters. Multifamily developers should monitor Back Bay, Waterfront, and Downtown Crossing for conversion/redevelopment opportunities and emerging residential premium.

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami civic center receives $193M construction permit; 257K SF, 8-story, part of 130-acre Miami Freedom Park
Inter Miami CF stadium anchor; $1B mixed-use project near Miami International Airport; $20M Metrorail pedestrian bridge
Completion targeted spring 2028; consolidates city admin; relocates from 1931 Art Deco City Hall; major urban placemaking
stories.summary: Miami's new civic center secured a $193 million construction permit and topped off on July 10, 2026. The 257,000-square-foot, eight-story building is part of Miami Freedom Park, a $1 billion, 130-acre mixed-use development anchored by Inter Miami CF's stadium near Miami International Airport. A $20 million Metrorail pedestrian bridge is under construction. The building will consolidate city government functions and is expected to complete by spring 2028.
stories.impact: Miami Freedom Park will be one of South Florida's largest mixed-use centers, creating a new live-work-play hub near MIA with stadium, civic functions, retail, and residential opportunity. The Metrorail connection and stadium anchor will drive foot traffic and property value appreciation across the broader 73-acre site. Multifamily developers should evaluate remaining parcels within the park for residential density plays; the civic anchor and transit access create strong residential demand fundamentals. Monitor planning/phasing to identify shovel-ready infill residential sites competing for or complementing Freedom Park residential nodes.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related California launches 700 Broadway, Santa Monica; three-building luxury residential; 70K SF curated amenities
Private 0.75-acre courtyard, rooftop resort-style (pools, hot tubs, sauna, BBQ); indoor Speakeasy bar; handmade materials
Lifestyle-focused luxury positioning; high amenity spend signals SoCal premium rental market appetite and design differentiation
stories.summary: Related California's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica features three-building luxury residential architecture (brick and glass/metal cladding) surrounding a private 0.75-acre courtyard and rooftop resort amenities including dual pools, hot tubs, sauna, and BBQ. The development includes 70,000 square feet of curated indoor and outdoor amenity space, including The Reserve (Speakeasy-style bar and recreation). Design emphasizes natural, handmade materials and California-inspired luxury-casual aesthetic.
stories.impact: 700 Broadway exemplifies the premium coastal multifamily market's shift toward experience-driven, lifestyle-centric positioning to justify SoCal urban rents and compete for affluent, discretionary renters. High amenity capital spend ($70K SF total) signals developer confidence in Santa Monica rental absorption and willingness to pay for experience premium. This raises the bar for competing Santa Monica/West LA projects; developers without equivalent amenity/design differentiation may face lease-up friction. Expect continued Santa Monica multifamily rent growth and tighter leasing timelines for best-positioned assets.

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach imposes 6-month moratorium on high-rise waterfront condo applications; effective Jan. 20, 2027
Applies to Flagler Drive corridor; cited rapid transformation and 2,000+ luxury condo units in pipeline
Zyscovich hired for zoning/economic analysis; signals concern over overdevelopment and demand for planning refinement
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners approved a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for high-rise waterfront properties in the downtown Flagler Drive corridor (Monroe Drive to Southern Boulevard, Flagler Drive to Washington Road), set to expire January 20, 2027. The pause follows rapid condo development; the city is hiring Zyscovich to conduct zoning and economic analysis and draft new regulations. Over a dozen luxury condo projects totaling 2,000+ units are in the works, with developers including Related Group, Terra, and BH Group.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach's moratorium is a critical supply-side signal. The city is pausing entitlements to assess overdevelopment risk, indicating pipeline congestion (2,000+ condo units) and concern about absorption, pricing, and market saturation. For multifamily developers: (1) this stalls new condo-tower approvals but may not affect rental multifamily zoning; (2) the moratorium creates a 6-month regulatory vacuum that could shift capital to competing Florida markets (Miami, Fort Lauderdale, Jacksonville, Tampa); (3) post-January 2027, revised zoning may favor rental over for-sale, opening rental development opportunities. Monitor Zyscovich analysis closely for zoning shifts; rental multifamily could gain relative advantage if condo supply concerns drive policy rebalancing.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing proposes 110-unit affordable at 295 South Mathilda Ave., Sunnyvale; 25% reserved for formerly unhoused
Former Wells Fargo bank site conversion; market-rate manager unit; high community support and Greenbelt Alliance backing
Sunnyvale multifamily pipeline expanding; Beam Reach also proposed 265-unit residential on Google-occupied land
stories.summary: Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing proposed a 110-unit affordable housing building on a former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale (295 South Mathilda Avenue), with 25 percent of units designated for previously unhoused individuals. All units except the manager unit are income-restricted; area median income for Santa Clara County is $143,850 (1-person household). The project has earned support from residents and community advocates. Separately, Beam Reach recently proposed a 265-unit residential conversion of a former Google office at 215 Bordeaux Drive.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale is emerging as a secondary multifamily hub as AI-driven job spillover from San Francisco intensifies housing demand in Silicon Valley. MidPen's affordable-focused project and Beam Reach's office-to-residential conversion both signal rising residential demand in Sunnyvale and weakening office absorption. For multifamily developers: (1) Sunnyvale offers lower land costs and fewer Bay Area regulatory barriers than SF/Oakland; (2) AI/tech job concentration in Santa Clara County (Meta, Apple, Google satellite offices) is driving renter in-migration; (3) mixed-income pipeline suggests public support for density; (4) monitor zoning changes and conversion economics as office-to-residential arbitrage continues. Strong opportunity window for mid-market multifamily before land prices / per-unit costs rise.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder ordered to pay $400M damages for fraud in luxury waterfront deal
Alleged $5B bond and European banker scam; land acquired from Energy Commissioning; never built; previous SEC enforcement
Real estate development fraud case; luxury waterfront project ('The District,' San Gabriel River) fraudulent from inception
stories.summary: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered by a Williamson County jury to pay $400 million in damages for breach of contract and fraud. The pair purchased land and engaged Energy Commissioning for $21 million to develop infrastructure for a 400-acre luxury waterfront development called 'The District' near the San Gabriel River, but the project was never intended to materialize. Krishnan and Gounder made only symbolic payments and attempted to secure a $5 billion bond while pitching European bankers on inflated plans. Both were previously targeted by the SEC in 2023 for an alleged Ponzi scheme.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on legitimate multifamily development. However, this case underscores reputational and regulatory risk in luxury waterfront development and highlights SEC scrutiny of developer financing claims. For multifamily operators: (1) due diligence on acquisition partners and joint-venture sponsors must include SEC/enforcement background checks; (2) luxury waterfront projects in Texas (Georgetown, San Gabriel River areas) are higher-fraud-risk given land-banking cycles; (3) European institutional capital chasing US real estate may be more vulnerable to false projections—fund transparency and deal sourcing rigor accordingly. Otherwise, a judicial outcome reinforcing fraud liability; not a market signal.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development plans 740-acre Wildhaven resort in Denton; 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), 100-key hotel, $350–$450M value
Nature-centric luxury: 6 miles horse trails, 10-acre bass pond, spa, restaurants; 400 acres developed, 300+ preserved
First Wildhaven location; subsequent resorts planned in Colorado, Florida; Denton growth corridor targeted
stories.summary: Sarasota-based An Ki Land Development is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury resort in Denton totaling $350–$450 million in development value. The project includes 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), a 100-key hotel, equestrian areas, restaurants, spa, and a 10-acre bass fishing pond. Each home sits on at least 1-acre lots; 400 of 740 acres will be developed, with 300+ preserved for nature. Noble Classic Homes is building homes in phase one; HKS is the design firm. Wildhaven Lifestyle Holdings plans additional resorts in Colorado and Florida.
stories.impact: Wildhaven signals luxury resort/lifestyle real estate (not rental multifamily) expansion into Denton, capitalizing on DFW population growth and Denton's emerging status as a destination. While positioned as luxury-resort/second-home product (not primary rental), the project reflects broader DFW infrastructure and amenity-driven development trend. For multifamily developers: Denton's rapid growth and improving amenities (new resort, equestrian, dining) enhance residential appeal and renter demographics. Monitor Denton rental fundamentals—if resort and lifestyle infrastructure attract affluent workers / residents, primary rental demand and rents will follow. This is a supply-neutral signal but a demand-positive bellwether for North Denton multifamily fundamentals.
